title: Programming Languages
subtitle: Different tools for different jobs

sections.content:
Just like there are many human languages (English, Spanish, Japanese), there are many **programming languages**. Each one was designed for a particular kind of work.

Some languages are great for building websites. Others are designed for analyzing data or working with databases. You don't need to learn all of them — most developers specialize in just a few.
sections.analogy: Programming languages are like tools in a toolbox. You could use a screwdriver to hammer a nail, but a hammer works much better. Similarly, you *could* build a website with Python, but JavaScript was built specifically for that job.
keyPoints: **HTML** — The structure of web pages (headings, paragraphs, images)
**CSS** — The styling of web pages (colors, fonts, layout)
**JavaScript / TypeScript** — Makes web pages interactive (buttons, animations, dynamic content)
**Python** — Popular for data analysis, AI, and automation
**SQL** — The language for talking to databases (storing and retrieving data)

sections.instruction: Drag each language into its primary category. Where is each language used most?

sections.question: You want to make a button change color when someone clicks it on a website. Which language would you use?
sections.explanation: JavaScript (and its cousin TypeScript) is the language that makes websites interactive. When you click a button and something happens — a menu opens, a color changes, data loads — that's JavaScript at work.

sections.content: Don't feel overwhelmed by the number of languages out there. Professional developers don't know all of them either. Most people start by learning **one language well**, then pick up others as needed.
sections.tip: If you're curious about web development, start with HTML, CSS, and JavaScript. If you're curious about data or AI, start with Python. You can always learn more later.

completionMessage: You now know the major programming languages and what they're used for. Remember: you don't need to learn them all. Next, we'll look at what actually happens when you visit a website.
